FT_IC1.evidence: Authors explicitly analyze data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, ABCD 4.0 release, baseline 2016-2018 (ages 9-10), 1-year follow-up 2017-2019, 11,962 children recruited from 21 U.S. sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: multivariable linear regression models examining prospective and cross-sectional associations between screen time modalities and MIND diet nutrition score in ABCD data (N=8,267).

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Academic Pediatrics (2024); no indication of duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 2 reports separate regression coefficients for texting (B=-0.15, 95%CI:-0.24,-0.05, p=.01) and social media (B=-0.16, 95%CI:-0.28,-0.05, p=.01) at Year 1, each paired with MIND diet nutrition sum score (a physical/dietary health outcome = individual-trait). Phone/SM-specific labels with own numeric estimates found in main results table.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple regression B coefficients with 95% CIs and p-values reported for texting, social media, and other modalities vs. MIND diet score in Tables 2 and 3.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential regression analyses conducted; numeric results reported for the SM/phone variable to individual-trait (nutrition) relationship throughout Tables 2 and 3.

FT_EC7_reason: SM/phone variables appear as IV (screen time predicting nutrition), not exclusively as DV. Furthermore, the paper includes both prospective and cross-sectional analyses with screen use as predictor. EC7 cross-sectional carve-out also applies: cross-sectional analyses with SM as IV exist throughout.
notes: Paper examines texting and social media (among other modalities) as IVs predicting MIND diet score. Step 1 of IC2 MET on texting and social media coefficients in cross-sectional analysis (Year 1 screen time x Year 1 MIND diet). INCLUDE.

FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional analysis of Year 3 of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, 5.0 release, N=9,859, U.S. participants (baseline 2016-2018, 21 recruitment sites).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis; multiple linear regression models estimating associations between transgender/gender-questioning identity and screen time modalities and problematic screen use scales in ABCD data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Annals of Epidemiology (2024); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 2 reports adjusted coefficients for social media time (B=0.83, 95%CI:0.06-1.61, p=.035), texting (B=0.81, 95%CI:0.04-1.59, p=.040), Social Media Addiction Questionnaire score/SMAQ (B=0.43, 95%CI:0.17-0.69, p=.001), and Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire score/MPIQ (B=0.34, 95%CI:0.07-0.61, p=.012), all as DV, with transgender identity as IV. Per v7 criteria, gender identity is explicitly classified as an individual-trait variable (not demographic), satisfying the other-side requirement. Phone/SM-specific labels (SM time, texting, SMAQ, MPIQ) with own numeric estimates confirmed.

FT_IC3.evidence: B coefficients with 95% CIs and p-values reported in Tables 2 and 3 for SM time, texting, SMAQ, and MPIQ against transgender/gender-questioning identity.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential linear regression analyses conducted and numeric results reported throughout Tables 2 and 3.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional, so condition (a) of EC7 (longitudinal design) is not met. EC7 does not apply.
notes: SM/phone variables are DVs here (transgender identity as IV predicting SM use), but gender identity qualifies as individual-trait per v7. Cross-sectional design. INCLUDE.

FT_IC1.evidence: Prospective cohort data from 9,398 participants in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, Years 2-3 (2018-2021), U.S. sample recruited from 21 sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis; logistic and linear regression models examining associations between bedtime screen use behaviors at Year 2 and sleep outcomes at Year 3 in ABCD data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Adolescent Health (2024); no indication of duplicate. (Note: a prior cross-sectional version in Sleep Health 2023 is cited as ref [19] and is a distinct analysis.)

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 3 reports separate regression estimates for 'Talk on the phone or text' as IV predicting sleep duration (B=-0.17, 95%CI:-0.22,-0.12, p<.001) and sleep disturbance (OR=1.13, 95%CI:1.05-1.23, p=.002), and 'Spend time online on social media' predicting sleep duration (B=-0.21, 95%CI:-0.26,-0.16, p<.001) and sleep disturbance (OR=1.10, 95%CI:1.01-1.20, p=.025). Sleep (duration, disturbance) is an individual-trait outcome. Texting/phone and social media are phone/SM-specific labels with own numeric estimates.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple regression coefficients (B, OR) with 95% CIs and p-values reported in Table 3 for texting/phone and social media vs. sleep outcomes.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential regression analyses conducted; quantitative results reported throughout Table 3 for SM/phone variable to sleep (individual-trait) relationship.

FT_EC7_reason: SM/phone variables (texting, social media) appear as IV predicting sleep as DV, not as DV-only. Condition (b) of EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis, which is not met here. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Prospective design (Years 2-3). Texting/phone and social media bedtime use are IVs predicting sleep outcomes (individual-trait). Step 1 MET on both. INCLUDE.
